comments.content:
A couple of questions about Buddhism:

1. Can Buddhism be practiced simultaneously with the GD system? Are there any serious counter-indications to the GD mage living by the Four Noble Truths and the Noble Eightfold Path and working as prescribed in that tradition, including doing the meditative work?

2. Does reaching the higher grades in the GD systems lead to states that can be compared to the Buddhist concept of enlightenment?

3. The central aim of Buddhism is to escape from the cycle of birth and death through a state of enlightenment achieved essentially via self-reflective consciousness and abstinence from harmful behaviors. Buddhism sees everyday experience as being wrapped up in suffering and claims to offer a way to escape it. How does this differ from the aims of the Western occult tradition?

4. For JMG (or anyone else in the commentariat): I’m fortunate enough to have a center near me that teaches Vipassana meditation, along with a good amount of Buddhist philosophy, so it isn’t just the isolated technique. Would this be safe to perform with the standard GD set of practices (LBRP + MP + discursive meditation + divination) without unbalancing any of the subtle bodies?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, the first question -- which you get around to answering further down -- is what kind of Buddhism you have in mind. There are scores of different denominations and traditions, which differ dramatically from one another. Now that I know that you're considering Theravada Buddhism -- that's the tradition that teaches vipassana -- I can answer you.

1) Allan Bennett, one of the leading members of the Golden Dawn back in its original heyday, was also a practitioner of Therevada Buddhism and took Buddhist monastic vows, but he stopped practicing Golden Dawn magic when he did this. I don't recommend mixing different spiritual systems; choose one and stick with it.

2) The grades themselves don't take you to those states, any more than accepting Buddhist initiation does. They give you the tools you can use to get there, if you work at it.

3) Western occultism by and large doesn't treat spirituality as an escape hatch from existence. It also doesn't agree with this notion that existence is 'wrapped up in suffering" -- suffering is one aspect, joy is another, and you can go on as long as you want to, but none of these characteristics define the universe. (You could as well claim that life is wrapped up in peevishness, or amusement, or what have you.) From the Western occult perspective, the universe has valid reasons for being here and so do you; instead of trying to run away from it, it makes more sense to embrace life and make the best of it.

4) I don't recommend this at all. Again, choose one system and stick with it -- that's the way to attainment. Once you've mastered one system, you can consider going on to explore others.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
IANA Buddhist, though I'm an interested observer. I think a lot of confusion has arisen from the translation of "dukkha" as "suffering", with its connotations of Life as a Vale of Tears. It makes much more sense if you take a broader view of the meaning - "unhappiness", "irritation" even "pissed-off-ness." Thus, either there are things in the world that we don't like and don't want, and/or there is an absence of things that we do like and do want. This upsets us, hence "suffering." But suffering doesn't have to be grand and tragic. It can be the boss you don't like, the things you hear on the radio, your inability to get your children to listen to you, even the fact that the book you really want by JM Greer doesn't seem to be available.
Non-duality (with which I'm more familiar) says essentially the same thing, and it's striking that Mouni Sadhu, for example, was clearly deeply influenced by that concept. In non-duality, once you understand that life isn't going to behave exactly as you want all the time, and "suffering" is therefore part and parcel of existence, you start to accept that situation, and stop struggling against things you can't change. This would include karma. Once you do that, "suffering" disappears. As I say, Sadhu seems to have found that approach consistent with western esotericism.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I've always disliked the translation of dukka to suffering myself as it doesn't explain very well what the Buddha was trying to get at. JMG is right. Life itself has no characteristic, suffering, joy, or whatever else.

I don't like the translation because suffering is a result of the kind of processes going on inside your body and brain. Doesn't matter if the trigger is something you're imagining as a possible future or remembering from the past or someone doing something ugly to you at that very moment. Since the process comes from your brain and body you're manufacturing it and refreshing it thus you can learn to biohack it and produce a different result no matter what the triggering event may be.

BTW - this is one reason why Roman elites often seriously disliked Stoics and other Greek philosophers. If you can biohack your own body and mind for absolute clarity (wisdom) and constant inner pleasantness (joyfulness) it makes you much less controllable to whatever the elites want you to do since nothing they offer can compete with what you've given yourself. That means you're a serious loose-canon to the Biz-As-Usual ethos that permeates the elite class and their enforcers.

Anyway...

If one 'accepts' things without doing actual daily practices (Modern Pop-Stoicism does this a lot in my opinion) the result is trying to manage one's experiences instead. Manage it as in pop-culture's admonishment one needs an 'attitude adjustment'. You're just trying to manage yourself after the fact. If you are having to manage yourself into acceptance of the suffering you're experiencing at that moment then it's already too late. Not doing real inner work to change yourself which entails changing the prana (chi) too which is the next-most dense layer of our makeup (using Yoga's framework, not Western Occult framework).

It's a psychological management technique which does nothing at all about the cause.

If you set up the right set of spiritual practices together then real acceptance (the kind the Buddha and other spiritual traditions around the world talk about) begins to take place because the impediments to clarity (the first tiny hints of the long road to higher wisdom) and joy are dissipating from all the sheaths. Your Will becomes less impeded too.


As for the Buddha's Four Noble Truths. I think his opening statement - life is suffering is a second-order statement. He stated it that way because he could take advantage of the widespread cultural and spiritual framework of the populace in those days.

Technically speaking...if I understand things right, the material plane is an impediment only in one sense - less possibilities (Limits). Higher levels of being have fewer of the limits that make up the material plane. Higher levels don't need and are just fine without the physical embodiment we have. They can take on physical embodiment but unlike humanity's stage of involution it would be a fully conscious choice through the entire process, not the compulsive, subconscious thing it is with most of humanity right now. That doesn't make physical embodiment bad or wrong. It just means that things that are available to higher levels are not available to the material plane.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
For Theravāda magic other than natural magic, you might take a look at (no particular order)

1) what are called the "powers" or supernormal powers (iddhi(s) or siddhi(s)) which are mentioned in the suttas and described in more detail in the Visuddhimagga (Path of Purification) especially chapters 12-13, though other things are also translated into English as "power" (eg bala) and iddhi can refer to powers other than supernormal ones.

2) some of the things the Buddha says in the suttas about goodwill/lovingkindness/metta meditation, notably that it would be impossible for supernormal forces to "derange the mind" of someone practicing it to a certain level of competence.

3) "protection rituals" (paritta(s)), sometimes based on metta.

Apparently, in the Buddha's time, there was a widespread belief that stating truths could have magical effect. This is shown in quite a number of places in the suttas, including in cases where reciting suttas themselves is taken as having magical impact, and it looks to me like that may be where parittas come from.

comments.title: Analog Zettelkasten, the Eclipse, and Astral Supplement to Judson Exercise
comments.content:
Good evening!

I don't know the proper salutation to use for an eclipse, but whatever malefic effects it has are minimal.

First, something to share: A while back I heard about the "Zettelkasten" method of note-taking used by the German sociologist Niklas Luhmann. I tried following the method using digital tools for awhile and found it somewhat helpful, but never really got into the groove. I decided to try it out with pen and paper, as originally designed, and shortly after, temporaryreality pointed me to https://scottscheper.com/ . I've found his material helpful for getting going with this analog note-taking/knowledge management technique, and the most exciting application has been using it to record my notes and thinking on occult matters. Hopefully some other folks will find this intriguing.

Secondly, a couple of questions:

1. Speaking of the eclipse, I read your Mundane Astrology post on tonight's lunar eclipse (thanks for that!). Other than the political and economic effects you described in the posts, are there any more personal malefic effects to look out for (or would that depend on your natal chart?)? If so, are there any kinds of precautions to take and/or activities to avoid? (My current magical knowledge, if needed: I have been working through the DMH for about a year now, and I do the SOP, Discursive Meditation, and Ogham divination daily, and a Grove ritual at least weekly, whenever I can find space away from my wife and daughter - I substitute the Judson Exercise when I can't get away).

2. My understanding is that the Judson Exercise is primarily etheric in nature, whereas the SOP is primarily astral. If that assumption is correct, then when using the Judson Exercise as a daily banishing ritual, are there any further steps for astral hygiene worth taking to make up for the lesser astral component to the exercise?

Thanks so much for your time and care,
Jeff

comments.title: Translation update
comments.content:
I suspect the books I sent are still in the mail. But you may be interested to know that I've just finished translating Carl du Prel's "Das Rätsel des Menschen: Einleitung in das Studium der Geheimwissenschaften" [The Puzzle of Humanity: Introduction to the Study of the Occult Sciences] from 1892 and will have it available for sale in early July. I thought this short excerpt from near the end might be of interest to the readership:

"[Transcendental psychology] teaches that the human is the materialisation of a transcendental subject, the embodiment of a supersensory being. As an essential part of nature the human is surely the epitome of what all of nature seems to be, which does not arise from nothing, but rather can only be the materialisation of an invisible world. So thought the mystics. The Bible calls this materialisation of our invisible being “expulsion from paradise”. We can retain the myth, only we will interpret in the sense of the occult sciences, as Philo, Origen, the cabbalists, Plotinus and Plato did in his idealist philosophy. Paradise precedes birth, and is the pre-existence. The fall from grace does not first follow birth, rather it is the earthly birth. It coincides with the expulsion from paradise, that is, through birth our transcendental existence becomes unconscious to us. The “coats of skins” with which God clothed the fallen humans are the earthly bodies of which they were ashamed when they recognised them, whereas they were not ashamed of the previous nakedness of their etheric bodies. Through the fall from grace, so says the Bible, death came into the world. Certainly, for materialisation is on account of the transience of all matter only temporal, and dematerialisation, death, must follow it. For just that reason we should use life to obtain such goods which outlast death. The programme of our life conduct is determined by the knowledge that we are capable of development beyond death."

It's a short book (about 100 pages), but what most struck me was du Prel's conviction that a bright future lay ahead for the occult sciences and that the facts of experience from somnambulism, animal magnetism, and spiritualism would trump materialism because of their ability to explain the unconscious and the soul. His argument seems to be that materialism focuses only on what the consciousness can be aware of and thus ignores the role of the unconscious.

My question for today is what happened to somnambulism? Was it renamed to something else and neutered the way animal magnetism was transformed into hypnotism, or have you already covered this in your history series on the occult in the US and I just missed it or wasn't paying attention? In other words, where do we see somnambulism these days?

replies.title: Re: Translation update
replies.content:
When animal magnetism (aka Mesmerism) was in vogue, a somnambulist was simply a magnetized (Mesmerized) subject, and (s)he was thought to be able to perceive truly, or even act effectively, beyond the usual limitations of time and space. In French practice, they were called somnambules, or sometimes lucides.

Once Spiritualism developed, many somnambulists rebranded themselves as "mediums." Nowadays, in some occult practices, it's called trance-work.

Of course, the same hard materialism that reduced animal magnetism to hypnotism, reduced somnambules to hypnotized subjects.

replies.title: Re: Translation update
replies.content:
Thanks, that clears things up.

Kerry

replies.title: Re: Translation update
replies.content: It’s sleepwalking.

replies.title: Re: Translation update
replies.content:
If you're Kerry, I'm delighted to say that they've just arrived today, and thank you very much for both of them! I"m also very pleased to hear that you're working on one of du Prel's works -- he's all but forgotten nowadays but was considered first-rate back in the day.

As for somnambulism, what Robert said. These days, as Anon notes, the term has been flattened out to mean ordinary sleepwalking.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
One other thing to add to what others have said that may or may not be relevant for you: most social media feeds create the same kind of fear that traditional news does, with an added dose of more personal social anxiety. If you're not ready to take a fast from social media platforms, a few steps you can do to make them less anxiety-producing:

1) Tweak the data-saving/recommendation settings so that they retain the minimum amount of data. For anything that doesn't require a login to view (like youtube), consider viewing in a private/incognito window that doesn't save any of your data at all
2) Check social media on your computer instead of your phone - the phone apps have a lot more development effort put into them to make them addicting
3) Avoid random scrolling through feeds - focus instead on reading specific individuals or specific communities that you wanted to read on your own, rather than following a recommendation by the platform's algorithm
4) Limit your social media access to specific, pre-planned times and durations. If you find this hard/impossible to stick with for a few days, reconsider whether you can manage a complete fast for a week or so
5) Recognize that different platforms cater to different predilicitions, and work out which ones you might have the most trouble with. If you constantly worry about knowing what's happening right now, Twitter might be especially bad for you. If you're worried about what other people think about about your image and social status, Instagram might be tough. If "people being wrong on the internet" infuriates you, look out for Facebook. And so forth.

Good luck finding healthy ways to deal with your anxiety!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Prayer
replies.content:
One very good book that is largely- though not exclusively- about prayer is "How to Experience God Without Religion" by Denis Poisson (many of the commentariat and readership will know Denis better as Foolish Fish, a YouTuber and Patreon creator who puts out a lot of material about occultism and magic, as well as many book reviews). It's a thought provoking and useful guide to extemporaneous prayer as a means of making contact with The Divine, however one experiences it. It's largely couched in language that might make the most intuitive sense to those sympathetic to Western Monotheism and/or Neo-Platonism on the one hand or Vedic/Taoist non-dualism on the other, but I imagine most spiritually inclined people could derive at least a few pearls from it regardless of their tradition.

Two good books about the Rosary to mention. 1.) The Way of the Rose by Clark Strand and Perdita Finn. A fascinating look at the rosary and Marian devotion from an explicitly NON Catholic (or even "Christian"in any conventional sense) family. Occultists and polytheists will likely find it fairly welcoming as a read. 2) The Secret of The Rosary by St Louis de Montfort. This one is super trad and super Catholic, but it's got some penetrating insights into the practice and benefits of the Rosary. I suspect at least some of those insights could be of use to anyone looking to deepen a devotional practice that involves prayerful recitation of traditional formulae.

Hope this is helpful to some extent!

comments.title: Hermetic Bob
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid JMG
I’ve been looking up ways to incorporate archangels from the Tree of Life into my daily practice and the easiest to find seems to be Damien Echols’ method from his Angels and Archangels book. His is simple, straightforward, uses the LBRP as a base, and makes a lot of sense (working bottom to top, left to right per evocation/invocation, archangels below you to protect from Clipoff etc). However, while searching for info on archangels and the ToL I happened across an image of the Norse ToL structure rather than the usual elaborate artwork. This made me look at the Kabbalah ToL in a different light. Hod, Geburah, Chesed, and Netzach matching the same colors of the Aa’s in the LBRP. Now I’m wondering how much merit there may be to invoking/evoking those Archangels corresponding to a more literal structure of the ToL in place of Raphael, Gabriel, Michael, and Auriel. The kicker is I’ve also experienced some kind of jump in consciousness in which I’m always in the moment now and my perception of time has changed, the trade off is I can no longer feel light as strongly as before, it’s almost as if I’m starting from ground zero again, so me just testing out this theory isn’t helpful right now. (Also I have a minute fear of Clipoff as well) I still can’t shake the feeling this may carry some water going forward. Any insight you can provide is greatly appreciated, perhaps about how set in stone the LBRP/IRP is or another more well suited direction I could take.

replies.title: Re: Hermetic Bob
replies.content:
This is a very common tangle for beginning-to-intermediate magical practitioners to get into: "This book says this, and that one says that, and I had an insight from over here, and now I don't know what to do!" You can work yourself into a fine state of paralysis this way.

The cure? Do the practice exactly as written in the book where you got it. Or, as W.E. Butler used to say, "Don't monkey with the buzz-saw!"

I mean this quite seriously. You can bring your inner development to a screeching halt by getting caught up in a frenzy of tinkering and uncertainty. Do the practice exactly the way Damien's book tells you, and set aside all variations until you've gotten the results you are after. That builds strength of will and also gets the best results.

replies.title: Re: Hermetic Bob
replies.content:
"Don't monkey with the buzz-saw!"
I've never heard it put quite like this before! But your point is well made. Guess I in all earnest should be doing the right thing and not only following lessons to the letter but keeping a journal of my insights for looking into once I have the proper knowledge and experience down the line.
Thanks for taking the time, you're the man JMG!

comments.title: Sacred Geometry Oracle and The Way of the Golden Section
comments.content:
Thank you for the Sacred Geometry Oracle and The Way of the Golden Section. I am very excited about getting to work through the sacred geometry drawings in particular. This one seems to resonate with me more clearly than other works have.

The simple explanation of what occultism is, coupled with some recent comments and thoughts about will and imagination, have left me finally feeling oriented in what exactly it is we are all doing here. As a solo practitioner it has been difficult to figure out which end of the wand goes up so to speak.

Magic as the way of participation.
Magic as applied philosophy.
Pentagrams from sacred geometry placing order in a space.

It feels like a primer that was needed. I always have a hard time when their is no general shape of the goal and even the most basic terms are cloaked in obtuse language. I understand that some of that is unavoidable, having to use metaphor and allegory to explain the world of spirit because we can’t quite perceive it directly.

In any case, I was wondering about Fractals and Cymatics particularly in the case of sacred geometry. I suppose a case could be made that the Golden Ratio/Golden spiral is a fractal, but I’m talking more about dendritic and wave patterns. I think those deserve a place in sacred geometry because they were “beyond infinite,” and that realization had an English mathematician checking himself into a mental hospital.

Cymatics too because they are a wave form made manifest. A vibration that quite literally makes a physical pattern. I think they were known by the ancients, some of the technology required isn’t all that crazy to get hold of. It also plays into “in the beginning was the word and the word was with God and the word was God.” Or Verb if you prefer Levi (Which I do.) John Stuart Reid on his talk “Secrets of Cymatics II,” mentions how the voices of spiritually oriented people often appear with a sevenfold pattern. I am currently looking for the note. How this ties into Magic for me is through the vibratory principle. If we found the note of the sevenfold pattern and tried to mimic it in vibrating words of power, we may “sync up” with those currents faster. Or quite literally changing our vibration. It is an interesting thought and of course different cultures have produced different Cymatic art, so not necessarily suggesting one size fits all…I’m suggesting an experiment. Instead of finding the vibration over time, what if we treated it a bit like singing and tried to match the vibratory patterns? What patterns would be good for what applications in magic?

I can’t tell if I’m onto something or have gone off the deep end. Basically if we are a product of mind and vibration then we would be in some sense a fractal cymatic potentially on a universal scale. It also has implications for interactions with “The music of the spheres.” Harmonics, etc. Is anyone interested in checking my math? Do other planets have Schumann resonances? Is this a known field of music magic, beyond Pythagoras and the mono chord?

replies.title: Re: Sacred Geometry Oracle and The Way of the Golden Section
replies.content:
Thank you! I'm glad you find them useful.

Fractals and cymatics were discovered in recent times, long after classic sacred geometry was mostly forgotten. Can they be integrated into sacred geometry? That'll depend on whether someone decides to tackle the project of integrating them. Nobody yet knows how the integration will work because it hasn't happened yet. That's one of the exciting things about the rebirth of the Pythagorean sciences -- there's a vast amount of unknown territory all around the little that's known.

replies.title: Re: Oil
replies.content: My Encyclopedia of Natural Magic covers some of the information. Much of it is hard to come by, however, because the ingredients in "condition oils" are usually trade secrets! There's a useful way around this, however. If you take any ordinary oil, such as olive oil, and soak herbs in it, the oil takes on the magical properties of the herbs -- so you can make oils for candle dressing for any purpose by choosing herbs, soaking them in the oil for a month or so, straining out the herbs, and using the oil on the candle.

comments.title: Surrender
comments.content:
Hi JMG

Is the concept of surrender only a Christian concept or do other traditions have a similar path? I'm not sure if this is your area of expertise could you please elaborate on what this entails?

Whatever ones spiritual path or practice aren't there built in protections or is this limited to a guru relationship?

When it comes to connecting with a deity can an individual be left waiting for some time even after continuous pray and charity? Is there a need for greater sincerity and devotion? Or do deities choose what humans to involve themselves with?

Thank you for responding and as always so grateful for your wisdom and effort you put into this site, ecosophia.net, and your books. King in Orange, Encyclopedia of Magic, & Monsters An Investigators Guide were informative, engaging, and enjoyable. I've learned a great deal.

replies.title: Re: Surrender
replies.content:
1) This isn't my area of expertise at all. I do know that Islam is even deeper into the concept of surrender than Christianity -- the word Islam literally means "submission" or "surrender" in Arabic.

2) No, there are no built-in protections, and a guru relationship can be just as bad as doing it yourself, depending on the guru. There are things you can do to keep yourself safe, but there are always risks/

3) Deities are people, not vending machines. They choose what they want to do.

replies.title: Re: Surrender
replies.content:
The word ‘Muslim’ (one who ‘submits’) is used in two overtly distinct ways in the Quran.

One, it refers to the adherents of the ‘religion’ of Islam as Revealed through Prophet Muhammad (pbuh).

And two, it refers to an individual who is ‘spiritually’ a ‘Muslim’, even though he/she may not adhere to the religion of Islam. Thus Abraham and Jacob are referred to overtly as ‘Muslims’ in the Quran (2:131-135); even though they predated Muhammad (pbuh) by centuries and naturally could not have been adherents of the religion of Islam.

Thus the mystics of Islam (Sufis) recognize that ‘Muslims’ are present in each and every corner of the world; at least in the spiritual sense, if not theologically. There are Christians who are ‘Muslims’ spiritually; Hindus, Buddhists, Jews, and so on. And having read your books and followed your own blog since a bit JMG, I would venture forth and suggest that there are good ‘Muslim’ Magicians out there as well.

Prayers and best wishes.
m

replies.title: Re: Surrender
replies.content: So noted, and thank you -- I don't claim to know the Quran at all. I've read an English translation but that was a very long time ago.

Have you ever heard of anything similar? I ask because I drink 4-6 cups of black tea with a splash of milk pretty much every day. I know you've said you are fond of green tea though, so it can't be that bad for the subtle senses!

Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Tea
replies.content:
Zen monks have long been notorious for their fondness for tea, and it apparently doesn't slow them down at all in their quest for enlightenment. I note also that back in the day, Druid groups in England had an officer whose duties included serving tea after the meeting. My experience is that tea has no negative effect on the subtle senses.

Pineal gland calcification is a real thing, but it's by no means clear whether or not it's a problem. The pineal gland is full of crystals anyway -- calcium phosphate and magnesium phosphate are among the minerals in the so-called "brain sand," which may interest users of cell salts -- and what I've been able to find out about calcification is that it consists of little crystals on the outside of the gland, which shouldn't interfere with the flow of hormones.

comments.title: Inventiveness
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

This is a strange one, but is there something in the astral tides which affects human inventiveness and ingenuity?

I ask because about 10 years ago, I would say I was at my peak inventiveness, and just could not stop thinking up ideas (even if they were wildly unrealistic). This has slowly tapered off, and I now struggle to think up anything new.

This may simply be an aspect of getting older, more cynical and critcally-thinking and growing out of my 20s, and I am now a lot more realistic in what I consider and more introspective in myself, but I can't help but feel like the 'atmosphere' for invention is no longer there. The same old flawed solutions (like hydrogen power) and re-hashes of decades old inventions keep being trotted out and everyone seems fresh out of ideas on intractable problems.

By the same regard, certain decades and even centuries have seen massive, rapid bursts of inventiveness, then a few decades (or centuries) of stagnation. Is this just the way it goes?

I read somewhere that there's evidence to suggest evolution undergoes the same process, with sudden leaps (like the Cambrian explosion) and then millenia of the new lifeforms settling in and sorting themselves out.
This is the premise behind a 'quantum leap' as well (that a quantum subatomic particle just suddenly jumps forward in space), so I guess it's a case of "as above, so below"!

replies.title: Re: Inventiveness
replies.content:
Okay, there are a couple of different factors here. First, in any field of study, you get all your new ideas in the first decade. If you want to have new ideas, take up some field of study you've never studied before -- for the next ten years, you'll get plenty of new ideas. That I know of, nobody knows why that happens, but it's very reliable.

Second, every civilization goes through periods of inventiveness and then settles down to elaborate what it's invented. Why that happens is another good question, but it's also reliable and doubtless ties into the life cycle of civilizations.

Third, yes, there are tides in the Unseen that tend to generate novelty and others that encourage the elaboration and development of what's already been invented.

So it's a complex matter! Yes, "as above, so below" is a good way to think about it.
